Sentence: You wake before the market does.
The world outside your window smells like the sea has pressed a palm against the town — salt and something mineral and honest.
Your jacket still has the salt ring
on the cuff from last night's walk along the Floodwall; the silver strand at your temple catches a streetlight and looks like a tiny lighthouse of its own.
The rolled map under your arm is damp
where it presses against you, and the edges smell faintly of old paper and ink and rain.
Across the boardwalk, under the lean shadow of a repair stall, Jonah Reyes is hauling a length of rigging.
Up close the waxed canvas of his vest smells of coffee and tar; even from here that
scent pulls at the hems of memory — mornings when the bay was calm, when the work was simpler, when he would grin and call you an alarmist for putting sandbags under a fence.
He meets
your eyes.
The amber in his gaze folds sunlight you haven't earned yet into something warm.
Your chest tightens.
Hope and the ghost of something like fear sit on top of each other like seafoam.
Morning, Solace.
(He hauls the coil and grins; the crow's-feet at the edge of his eyes deepen.)
Morning.
(You tuck the map tighter against your ribs so he can't see the pencil marks listing pilot locations.)
You look like you're carrying the tide itself.
You alright?
(He pauses, the coil draped over one shoulder.)
I'm fine.
Busy is fine.
(You laugh; it comes out too breathless.
You don't want to let him see how many times you've rehearsed asking for help.)
Busy suits you.
We're at the old south mooring later if you want a look.
I'm fixing a float—might be able to jury-rig it into something that lasts through spring.

There are so many small openings in Jonah Reyes's voice — a map of possibilities — and you find yourself measuring which ones you can step into without tripping over promises you don't yet have the authority to make.
• Wave and keep walking
You give him a small, tight wave and keep moving.
The motion feels safe — you preserve the neatness of plans you haven't told anyone about.
Jonah calls after you, uncertain, but you keep your back to the morning and it softens the edge of adrenaline.
• Stop and ask about the float
You stop.
Your map tucks against your side like a shield.
'Tell me about the float,' you say, letting your curiosity be the pretext.
He brightens, launches into lines about reclaimed foam and rope splice techniques, and your chest loosens a degree; collaboration smells like tar and schematics and something like home.
Continue at The Saltworks — Exterior to Interior | Dawn
You cross the market as the first stalls unzip.
Smoked fish steam fogs your breath.
The Saltworks door opens with a creak that has already become a domestic punctuation in your life.
Inside, cool diffuse light
pools across benches.
Seedling trays sit like small green cities on long tables.
Prototype berms — small ridges of sand, root fiber, and planted cordgrass — glint with residual moisture.
Your fingers brush damp soil; dirt
makes little maps on your thumb.
If I angle them like this, the light will simulate cloud cover earlier...
and—you'll see, the sensors will read different evapotranspiration.
(She smiles without looking up, the kind of grin that could short-circuit a spreadsheet.)
You crouch beside the trays, running a fingertip along a tender stem.
The seedlings bend, then spring back like a promise.
How are the sensors reading?
Temperate.
Good.
Raff sent drone footage overnight — his angles actually make the new terracing look cinematic.
(She gestures to a tablet.
Her hands are streaked with potting mix.) 'If Ben sees that footage, it's a narrative win.'
(You take the tablet and study the footage.
From the drone's silent eye, the terraces look heroic — long lines of panels like a staircase to the sky, native grasses waving in sync.)
(Lowering her voice) 'You rehearsed the agenda?'
Three pilot sites, phased trials, co-management framework, community oversight clause.
I want to be careful with language — 'managed retreat' is a phrase that closes doors.
(You feel each word like a bead on a line.)
You're threading a needle, Marin.
(Her voice goes soft.) 'But people listen when you don't shout.
You make practical things sound like honor.'
she says the quiet truth when the town needs it.
• Offer to help with the LEDs
You kneel and take the strip Asha hands you.
Your hands are clumsy at first; she corrects your angle, laughing.
The small tactile work centers you — solder-scent, warm plastic, the satisfying click when a connector snaps into place.
• Refuse, keep checking seedlings
You shake your head and slip back to the trays.
'Go ahead,' you tell Asha, and she tosses you a grateful look.
The seedlings' green demands attention; responsibility hums at the base of your skull like a tide.
Continue as Raff's message pings in
Dropped the footage live.
No caption — organic reach climb.
Also, put 'Save Our Shorelines' on a sticker mockup.
People like stickers.
(Half-smiling) 'Leave the viral to you.
I'll handle the wording.'
Cool.
Also—Elder Mae dropped off something for you.
Said you might need a sugar boost before the meeting and a piece of history attached.
Salted lemon tart?
(You glance up, expecting a small paper bag.
The idea alone warms you.)
Yeah.
Also a story, apparently.
She left a note: 'History neuters hubris.'
history neuters hubris.
So many of your designs are answers to hubris—concrete promises or quick fixes.
Elders and tides both remind you about what remains when the noise dies down.
Elder Mae arrives before Ben.
She moves with a slow certainty, braid over her shoulder.
Her shawl smells of dried seaweed and lavender.
She sets the tart on the table with the kind of ceremony that
here is sustenance, and here is a lesson.
When I was a girl, the first storm took Mr.
Hargrove's skiff clean out of his hands.
He sat on the pier and watched it go, and the tide took his swear-words away.
(She looks at you.) 'We built then.
We built without asking the sea to join us.
Remember that before you build.'
You always tell it so plainly.
(You reach for the tart, but hesitate because the metaphor still tastes of salt.)
Plainness is mercy, child.
And if you can make mercy into something people can use—legally, physically—then you will have done right.
(You bite into the tart.
The lemon is sharp; the salt brightens the flavor into something that feels like sun on a cold morning.
Your plan for the meeting proves itself more palatable with sugar in your mouth.)
Ben's office called.
He says he'll be late — traffic and a council briefing.
But his assistant said they read Raff's footage.
That's good.
People like a story they can picture.
We have to make the story ours, not just pretty footage.
The pilot's technical details, but framed as stewardship: who keeps the mooring, who tends the berms, how funding goes into wages not into spectacle.
You know the line.
Practical, principled.
Not idealistic, not conciliatory.
(You set the agenda between two coffee cups.
The words feel like anchors: pilot, co-management, community oversight.
You run timings on your fingers — ten minutes for introductions, fifteen for the berm demonstration, twenty for question time.
Your voice rehearses itself in the margins.)
Don't forget to ask for what you need.
(She taps her index finger on the paper.) 'A request is not a weakness.'
I know.
Then ask for it plainly.
Ben Rhee has not yet appeared, and that absence hums with possibility.
His visit could mean formal recognition, funding, politics folded into pavement.
Or it could mean compromise, the kind that smells like new concrete and leaves a bitter aftertaste.
practical, principled.
• Practice the opening line aloud
'We propose a pilot series that centers local knowledge and pays local hands.' Asha nods.
Raff claps once.
The room feels incrementally steadier.
• Sit quietly and visualize the meeting
faces leaning forward, the mural behind the council table, the hum of the ventilation system.
You place the pilot sites like pebbles on a map in your head, and the visualization calms your pulse like a tidal rhythm.
Continue as Raff mentions Jonah and preparations
(Tapping the tablet, excited) 'Also—Jonah's going to swing by after the market.
Might be handy.
He owes me a favor for that rigging tutorial.'
(Your heart—absurdly—hops.
You were planning to ask him for help retrofitting a mooring after the meeting.
Now the possibility is a hinge under your breath.) 'Good.
Tell him to bring his splice kit.'
Told you.
Small towns are small worlds.
Also, Ben's assistant said to expect some questions about liability.
Be ready.
Liability is paperwork.
People are the real problem.
Paperwork can kill a project as surely as a storm.
(She leans in.) 'We'll have the co-management clause drafted.
We'll suggest an independent oversight body.
We make room for fishermen on the pilot's steering committee.'
And wages for maintenance.
(You write it down in bold.)
And a clause for adaptive management — if something fails, we convene and fix rather than double down.
(You imagine the steering committee around a table, fingers pointing at tide charts, voices steady.) 'Okay.
We can do this.'
You stand, the tart half-eaten on the plate, a map in your arm, words in your mouth.
The air in the Saltworks feels charged — not with panic but with that clean surge that comes when
a town begins to move together.
You have rehearsed the line between practical and principled until it's comfortable in your mouth; you have allies; the footage is in circulation; Elder Mae has given you food and
history and a nudge.
In the next breath, you decide you will ask Jonah Reyes to help retrofit one of the old moorings after the meeting.
You smooth your jacket and step toward the door, tongue tasting lemon and salt and the bright metal of intention.
You push the door closed behind you with the same deliberate calm you learned when storms taught you to move slow and steady.
The tang of kelp preserves lingers under the colder metallic tang of the
a map of brine and sugar and the proof that
food, like policies, must be tasted before you promise it to people.
You hang your heavy-weather jacket over a chair.
The bracelet of recycled polymer beads glints when you gesture; for a heartbeat it catches the light like a small, stubborn lighthouse.
You pull a fresh hydrograph down
from the roll and press the paper flat against the chalkboard, thumb leaving a salt-dark smudge on the margin.
All right.
If we open with the cost comparison, people will hear money before ecology.
I want them to feel the shore first—then the numbers.
Good.
Start with the lived line—Elder Mae's photo, the old marsh cross-section, then the drone still of the terraces.
Make it visceral.' (She taps a sticky note and slides it beneath the chart.) 'Then hit them with short, clear numbers.
'Maintenance wages' in bold.
No euphemisms.
hydrographs, annotated cross-sections, hand-drawn isobaths where living shorelines will sit.
Your fingers want the clean sweep of ink across the board, but your brain catches on details.
Tide
variance here.
Shoal drift there.
You taste lemon and salt in the back of your mouth—leftover from the tart you abandoned in the last meeting—and it steadies you, oddly like a talisman.
These cuts are gold.
From above it looks like art.
People will eat this with forks if we don't stop them.' (He rewinds and points with his finger on the screen.) 'See that pattern at 0:14?
If you shift the southern array, you reduce stress on the moorings by a measurable amount.

That's exactly what we need—something that makes people's chests open before their spreadsheets kick in.' (You mark the frame with a sticky note.) 'Can you run versions with and without the southern shift?
Keep them under twenty seconds.
On it.
Also—Ben's assistant pinged.
He wants to see the permit language by noon.
And Lydia Voss's team is touring the levee sites this afternoon.
I thought you'd want to know.
precise, non-confrontational, impossible to misread.
Permits.
Conditions.
A
timeline you can stand by.
You feel Asha's eyes on you as you send it.
Ben's wording will be cautious.
He always is.
But you know your line—co-management, maintenance wages, adaptive clauses.
Don't let them sand it down.
We won't let them.
If Ben trims language, we'll push for review clauses.
It's safer politically and ethically.
You sound like a lawyer when you're excited.
Focus, Marin.
• Ask Raff to emphasize human stories in the cut
Raff's grin gets bigger.
'Already on the storyboard—Mae's bench and Jonah's dock will be the two anchors.' He shoots you a thumbs-up.
You feel the pitch tighten into a human arc.
• Ask Raff to prioritize technical clarity for Council
Raff's fingers pause over the timeline.
'Okay—I'll make a second edit with overlay metrics and the maintenance schedule.
It'll be dry, but Council will eat it.' He leans forward, serious, and you feel the campaign's backbone settle under your ribs.
the language of memory and the language of oversight.
Asha's hand finds yours on the edge of the bench in a quick, grounding squeeze.
the language of memory and the language of oversight.
You let the immediate
tug to emphasize stories stay in your throat; Asha's hand finds yours on the edge of the bench in a quick, grounding squeeze.
You pin a new set of talking points to the board.
Each phrase is measured; you trim adjectives with a curator's patience.
'Ecological outcomes,' 'long-term cost savings,' 'local stewardship,' 'transparent oversight.' You choose verbs that mean work—'maintain,' 'adapt,' 'assess'—not promises.

—we propose a pilot of three living terraces, anchored at the shoal, monitored quarterly, employing local crews for maintenance and enabling continued access for small-scale fishing.' (You look at Asha.) 'Short, human.
No abstract future-tense.
Don't shy away from the good news.
People respond to competence that feels like care.' (She taps the line about wages.) 'Leave this in.
He's trying to bridge us and Lydia Voss's timeline.
Good.
We'll meet him halfway on documentation, and keep the co-management clause non-negotiable.
I'll draft the clause language.
Keep it short, enforceable, and with a built-in review period.
You spend the afternoon alternating between drafting the community handout and tweaking the proto-terrace markers in the model bay.
Each tray of marsh plugs slides into the misting rig with a careful, satisfying thud.
Your hands
are damp and cold, but you press each tray in like setting a small promise into place.
The mist cools the back of your neck; it smells faintly of peat and fresh water, and you imagine
the living roots finding purchase in the shoal, catching sediment like slow, patient teeth.
I didn't take long.
You looked like you could use a spare cleat and an opinion.
You mapped that?
That could change placement for the terraces.
Felt like it.
The sand's been migrating west since last winter.
If you hug the terrace closer to the older breakwater, the current slows and the sediment will settle sooner.
Keeps the mooring lines less taut—less wear.' (He taps a sketch.) 'Also—brought the cleat.
Thought you'd want to test retrofit suggestions on the actual bolt pattern.
knots, load dispersal, how roots will tangle with rope.
It isn't all work;
there are soft interruptions—an offhand joke from Jonah about your 'obsessive margins,' a joke about Raff's editing making everyone a poet for ten seconds.
You push and prod at assumptions together, and each push is a
small mutual shaping.
If you want, I can be at the docks early tomorrow.
I'll bring the kids who helped me with the scouting—give them a look at real-world tech.
Might help with buy-in.
That would be perfect.
Local faces, local knowledge.
We need to show people this is their work, not just our plans.
Cool.
Also—Raff's footage looked amazing.
You're getting good at this, Sal.' (He searches your face, then adds quieter.) 'You look tired, though.
You okay?
I'm...holding.
Tonight's a long one, but the finishing line is clearer than it was this morning.
Good.
Let me know if you want someone to run lines with you before the hearing.
I can be clumsy with a mic, but I can tell a story about why the sea matters.
• Ask Jonah to run lines with you tonight
Jonah's face brightens; he folds himself into the conversation with an ease that makes rehearsal feel playful.
He reads your opening like a poem, then stumbles and laughs, and you both find a cadence that feels honest.
• Save the rehearsal for morning and focus on the handout
Jonah nods, understanding.
'Okay—morning then.' He helps you tape the final handout instead, his fingers careful around the paper, and you feel the work settle—less performance, more craft.
not managerial, not rhetorical, simply solid.
not managerial, not rhetorical, simply solid.
a small, careful motion—press, slot, click.
The rig exhales and the droplets catch the board light and throw
tiny, jewelled reflections across the room.
You trace, with a finger from a pen, the route you will take when presenting the prototypes—where you'll pause to point, which anecdote you'll give for each terrace, when you'll
defer to fishermen, when you'll call on Elder Mae.
If we keep the language simple and the stakes visible, they can't mistake our intentions.
You did the work, Marin.
The rest of tonight is polish.
Let the community see themselves in it.

You look around the Saltworks—at the ragged chalkboard, the lined hydrographs, the mug with a lipstick stain, the jar of kelp preserves half-empty—and you feel the rightness of it, like the fitting of a key.
Someone else's applause no longer feels like a verdict; it feels like feedback.
machines, the fridge in the corner, the soft sound of rain beginning to
smear the windows.
Outside, lights wink on along the boardwalk.
The levee lights far beyond throw a line of small, steady beacons across the wet horizon.
with work you can show and words you can file.
With a plan you can put in people's hands.
You close the door and breathe, feeling the finality of tonight's prep and the forward pull of tomorrow's hearing.
The ready weight of the handout sits in your satchel like a small, heavy promise.
not a demand, but an invitation to protect what everyone has already been saving.
the folded handout inside like a small, deliberate promise.
The memory of the Saltworks — chalk dust under your nails, Asha’s tired satisfied smile, the hummed
promise of machines — is still under your skin.
It steadies you as you step beneath the bulbs and the gull-light.
a town meeting and a stage.
The front row of folding chairs is packed with fishermen and shopkeepers,
kids on laps, the old and the new squeezed together.
Jonah is there, anchored in the first row.
His fingers drum a slow, familiar rhythm on a rusted whistle hung at his throat; when he catches
your eye he gives a small, crooked lift of his chin — not quite a smile, but a shore-line gesture of faith.
The air smells of wet wood, smoked fish, and the metallic tang of the sea.
The bulbs smell faintly of hot filament and salt.
Conversation threads and then hushes as Councilor Ben Rhee steps forward, coat collar turned up against the drizzle.
Good evening.
Thank you all for coming out.
Tonight we hear proposals.
We'll start with Marin Solace, who will present the cooperative's plan.
• Finger the map edge and breathe slowly
You press the rolled map to your ribs, let the breathing slow.
The rhythm grounds the words you practiced into something you can release into the air.
• Glance at Jonah once, then look away
You look at Jonah, catch the steadying set of his jaw, and the look fills you with a warmth that steadies your fingers; then you look back at the podium and let the heat settle into resolve.
Continue
You step to the podium.
Rain beads on the lectern and skitter down to the boards.
Your voice comes out plain, technical, human — the voice you've rehearsed in the Saltworks in front of a chalkboard of hydrographs and stubborn hope.
Havenpoint is not a project.
It’s a network of places, people, and practices.

What I'm proposing tonight is a phased, community-led adaptation: permeable terraces along vulnerable lots, marsh revetments at key inlets, and a maintenance program paid for with local hiring and adaptive governance.
These are low-carbon, reversible interventions.
They keep access to the shore for fishers, maintain the tidal exchange our eelgrass needs, and create jobs rebuilding what storms have taken.
Audience Member (voice roughened by years on the water) How long 'til it works?
We need protection now—what if the next surge hits before your marshes settle?
That's the right question.
These systems aren't instant like concrete, but they start dampening wave energy in the first season if properly sited, and they adapt.
They buy us time and repair ecosystem function that supports our fisheries — the very thing we need to preserve our livelihoods.
a sodden shoreline, then a living fringe holding with roots and promise.
The screen
draws sympathetic looks — faces soften when they see neighbors whose livelihoods the plan protects.
(whispered, urgent) 'If they ask about maintenance budgets, mention the co-management clause.
We wrote it in.'
Yes.
Co-management with fishermen, with transparent oversight, with adaptive funding tied to measurable outcomes.
(calm, voice carrying) 'Good evening.
The scenarios Marin described are thoughtful—and they have merit in a controlled setting.
But the models I run for regional resilience show we must reduce failure risk quickly.
A
rapid, engineered seawall—strategically placed and funded now—offers immediate reduction in flood risk for critical infrastructure.'
a promise of speed, of numbers, of investor confidence.
Her diagrams on the pavilion displays are bold, tidy sections.
She speaks in timelines and cost-benefit analysis; her cadence is a pulse the municipal apparatus understands.
A seawall provides a hard line, but it alters tidal exchange and can accelerate erosion elsewhere.
We must ask: who bears the downstream costs?
Who loses access to forage and moorings?
Our plan seeks to reduce harm and keep the sea and our people in conversation.
(a brief, almost imperceptible smile) 'Optimism is admirable.
But communities need certainty.
Investors need a clear timeline.
My proposal yields measurable flood reduction within months and secures investor funds that can be deployed now.'
Ben Rhee clears his throat, fingers on the microphone.
His face is careful, the mediator between urgency and principle.
We'll open the floor to questions.
Marin, Lydia—both of your models will be considered.

But council must decide whether to pursue community-led pilots with conditional funding and oversight, negotiate a hybrid plan with Lydia’s investor group, or accept Lydia's rapid seawall plan for immediate protection.
Let's hear from the community.
An elderly fisher rises, voice hoarse We can't wait years to feed our kids.
How do we pay the bills if the boats keep getting wrecked?
where groins would be lowered, how volunteer crews could be trained, what the first-year costs look like with conditional grants and phased disbursements.
If we work this way, can I keep my moorings?
My father's nets?
Or is that the cost?
(lowering your voice) 'You keep what you can work.
The pilots prioritize access at fishing points, designed with the very people who know how the bay breathes.
We plan for retrofits that keep boat lanes navigable.'
Good.
'Cause I trust you, but I trust the water more.
His laugh is a soft thing, and it makes people smile.
The mood lifts.
There's an energy to the room that feels like embers coming back to flame.
• Offer a prototype cutting to Elder Mae
Elder Mae takes the cutting with a small, surprised smile.
Her fingers—stooped but steady—test the mud and nod.
She says, 'Tend it true.' The exchange feels like a blessing handed back to the town.
• Hand the cutting to the fisher who asked about moorings
He takes it with a rough laugh, eyes glossy.
'Maybe my grandson'll see a better shore,' he says.
You both feel the weight of that hope and it steadies the next answer you give.
Continue
Lydia Voss stands to rebut in the formal period.
She points at timelines and risk matrices.
Her answers are crisp, efficient; the audience hears the sound of emergency—fast funding, quick builds.
She outlines contractor guarantees and a maintenance fund drawn from investor escrow.
We can mobilize crews within weeks, secure long-term insurance, and protect the downtown core.
There is cost, yes—but compared to catastrophic failure, it is investment, not expense.
The choice is between decisive action now or a slower, more uncertain path.
fear of immediate loss, hunger for certainty, and a steadier hope that something better can be grown instead of poured.
You speak again, softer, filling a technical detail with human consequence.
Decisions like this shouldn't force a zero-sum between safety and stewardship.
The pilots are designed to be auditable; if they fail, funding pivots.
If they succeed, we scale.
That’s conditional funding.
That’s accountability and local jobs.
Raff's footage loops again, this time with close-ups of hands — hands like yours and Jonah's, setting reeds, tying burlap, painting a mural on a floodwall.
Faces in the footage look back at the crowd, and the crowd recognizes neighbors.
The film elicits a swell of supportive applause.
Ben lifts a hand, calling for order with a patience that is almost prayerful.
We've heard both perspectives.
This council must recommend direction.
Marin, Lydia, can you both briefly state the core trade-off we must accept?
The trade-off is time versus process.
Lydia offers time—front-loaded protection—but at the cost of centralized control and potential ecological trade-offs.
We ask for process: slower, more participatory, but with deeper social and ecological resilience.
The trade-off is risk.
My plan reduces near-term risk to critical assets.
The cost is social consultation, which we commit to, but the window for decisive protection narrows every high tide.
You look out and see Jonah again — his jaw set, eyes steady, the light catching a bright line of salt at the temple.
You feel the room tilt toward possibility; the air is charged not with dread, but with the kind of tension that comes before change takes shape.
the council's framing question.
Ben folds his hands and looks down the rows before the microphone.
Council will now deliberate on a recommendation.

The options are: pursue community-led pilots with conditional funding and oversight; negotiate a hybrid plan with Lydia’s investor group; or accept Lydia’s rapid seawall plan for immediate protection.
We ask for your input.
Your chest is full of the Saltworks’ late-night meetings, of Asha's prototypes, of Raff's edits, of Elder Mae's quiet insistence that history matters.
You also hold the silence Lydia fills with the glow of contract-ready certainty.
You stand where policy and people meet, and you feel, more than ever, how much this town depends on the choice made tonight.
You step off the path with your satchel under your arm, palms slick with yesterday's tide.
The rolled-up updates bump against your ribs like a small, earnest pulse.
Light skips across the panels; water beads on
the mock berms and beads along the seams of the testing frames.
The terraces smell of sun-warmed salt and crushed grass, the tang of new growth mixed with hot silicon beneath your boots.
A polite cluster has formed near the liaison's clipboard—Ben at the edge, Lydia at a measured distance, Asha already elbow-deep in a sample tray, Raff fidgeting as he live-streams with one hand.
Elder Mae stands a little apart, braid knotted, watching as if reading tides in people instead of water.
You hand the liaison the conceptual integration packet.
Her fingers are cool; the corner of her mouth lifts when she sees the annotated diagrams you stuck into the margins.
Grant Liaison These overlays of panel siting and marsh berms—it's elegant.
It reduces footprint while adding habitat connectivity.
Did you run the wave overtopping models for the platform alignments?
You can feel how much is riding on each syllable; your chest tightens, then loosens.
This is what you built the nights for—the small drawings that could be read by a funder and by a boatwright alike.
We did.
The adaptive margin in the models lets us move panel arrays seasonally while retaining continuous sediment flow to the marshes.
Asha's test substrates do the heavy lifting on root establishment.
And they don't mind getting their feet wet.
The council's been impressed with the conditional approach—pilot scale with community oversight.
It gets us momentum without committing the town to a single solution.
It's sensible.
Scaled pilots reduce political risk and still allow for an investment pathway.
I'll reserve detailed critique for the meeting materials.
Her praise is thin but present; for a moment the terraces feel brighter for it.
Hey, Marin—people are lining up at the quay.
Old Mrs.
Hargrove brought her knitting.
This will trend.
Elder Mae lifts a posture like an old kelp frond catching sun, and then smiles—small but full—and steps forward with a braid of blue ribbon.
She threads it through the prototype marker post, hands steady despite age and weather.
You make good things, child.
We remember how to tend them.
A warm ache blooms in your ribs—vindication that tastes like sea-salt caramel.
All the hours, the paper, the patched samples—folding into this public, slow-blooming thing.
• Give the liaison a loose, grateful smile and share a quick personal story about the cooperative
You let your shoulders drop and tell her about the neighbor who taught you how to read a tide chart—how small acts kept the pilot alive.
She laughs, attention softens, and the liaison leans in, scribbling notes that feel like small promises.
• Maintain a focused, professional tone and walk through the models step-by-step
You tighten your shoulders into a practiced cadence, walking her through the technical overlays.
Her pen moves faster; you see in her eyes the math aligning with values.
The warmth is quieter, but it lands as seriousness rather than sentiment.
Scene continues
Jonah Reyes looks up as you approach; a smear of seawater runs down his forearm and he wipes it with the back of his hand, grinning like he's been told a good joke.
By the look of those floats, they're holding.
You hear that?
That's not just bobbing—that's weight distribution doing its honest work.
You crouch beside him, fingers finding the familiar knot of a cleat.
The rope is warm where the sunlight hits it, gritty with salt.
You anchor a testing float as Jonah instructs, his hands fluent, patient, steady in a way that quiets the nervousness you didn't know you were carrying.
Asha's substrate's sealing the seams.
If we can scale the material for more units, the maintenance cycle drops by—
—and fish have places to hide.
They like structure.
I snagged a juvenile snapper near the test last tide.
Proof's in the water.
You both laugh, but the laugh stretches into something softer.
His eyes—amber and open—hold a steady encouragement.
You feel seen in a way that's not about the grant or the cameras; he's anchoring you as much as the float.
You don't need to carry all of this alone, Marin.
Hand off a rope now and then.
Let someone else take the strain.
I know.
I'm learning how.
Raff runs up, breathless, waving his phone like a flag.
Media's here.
Live!
They want footage of the floats and a soundbite.
